title: Benchmark Brown Dwarf Systems I: Chemical Abundance Analysis of FGK Stars with Wide-Separation Brown Dwarf Companions Using PEPS
comment: Accepted to ApJ
summary: We present results from a spectroscopic survey of 32 FGK stars hosting brown dwarfs, using high-resolution optical spectra (R = 130,000 and 50,000) obtained with the PEPSI spectrograph on the Large Binocular Telescope. The primary goal of this survey is to determine precise stellar parameters and abundances for 11 elements (C, O, Mg, Si, Ca, Al, Ti, Fe, Y, S, and N) in these systems. We employ spectral synthesis within the BACCHUS framework to derive precise stellar properties and elemental abundance ratios. For our average S/N $>$ 200 data, we achieve a typical error of 42 K in T$_\mathrm{eff}$ and $\sim$0.03 dex for [Fe/H]. We observe a significant dispersion from a solar C/O ratio among the sample of brown dwarf host stars that host primarily wide-orbit brown dwarfs. Using established theoretical chemical frameworks, we discuss the implications of the observed Mg/Si and Ca/Al ratios for cloud properties in the brown dwarf companions. Finally, we evaluate the applicability of the [Y/Mg] stellar clock for our sample and discuss the broader implications of our results. This work provides a timely and uniform abundance analysis of host stars, supporting extended wavelength brown dwarf observations in the era of JWST.

AI.tldr: Spectroscopic survey of 32 FGK stars hosting brown dwarfs using PEPSI on the LBT; derived precise stellar parameters and abundances for 11 elements via BACCHUS spectral synthesis; achieved ~42 K Teff precision and ~0.03 dex [Fe/H] uncertainty; found significant C/O dispersion in wide-orbit brown dwarf hosts; discussed cloud property implications from Mg/Si and Ca/Al ratios; evaluated [Y/Mg] stellar clock; provides uniform abundance analysis supporting JWST-era brown dwarf observations.
AI.motivation: To determine precise stellar parameters and elemental abundances (C, O, Mg, Si, Ca, Al, Ti, Fe, Y, S, N) for FGK stars hosting brown dwarfs, enabling investigation of chemical trends and cloud properties in brown dwarf companions.
AI.method: High-resolution optical spectroscopy (R=130,000 & 50,000) with the PEPSI spectrograph on the Large Binocular Telescope; spectral synthesis using the BACCHUS framework to derive stellar parameters and elemental abundances for 11 elements across 32 FGK host stars.
AI.result: Typical uncertainties of 42 K in Teff and ~0.03 dex in [Fe/H] at S/N > 200; significant dispersion in C/O ratios among wide-orbit brown dwarf hosts; observed Mg/Si and Ca/Al ratios relevant to brown dwarf cloud properties.
AI.conclusion: The uniform abundance analysis supports extended-wavelength brown dwarf observations in the JWST era; [Y/Mg] stellar clock applicability evaluated; chemical ratios provide insights into brown dwarf atmospheric cloud properties.

title: Intrinsic iron abundance spreads in globular clusters
comment: 12 pages, 9 figures, and 3 tables. Table 1 only on CDS
summary: Globular Clusters (GCs) host multiple stellar populations, likely composed of a subset of FG stars that enriched the intracluster medium and gave rise to SG stars, each characterised by distinctive chemical patterns. These patterns typically include enhancements in elements such as N, Na, and Al, coupled with depletions in C, O, and Mg in SG stars. Traditionally, heavier elements such as those in the Fe-peak were considered unaffected in most GCs. However, recent studies have reported significant internal spreads in these elements, suggesting a more complex picture of chemical enrichment within GCs. This study seeks to derive precise and homogeneous differential iron abundances in a large sample of GCs. By doing so, our aim is to investigate the presence of intrinsic iron spreads within them and to assess the existence of differences in Fe between their stellar populations. We used the Python-based tool Q2 to determine both differential stellar parameters and iron abundances for 92 sibling stars, defined by the similarities in their stellar parameters, across 13 Galactic GCs. This differential approach reduces the influence of non-LTE effects, and minimises observational errors, and systematic biases linked to stellar parameters. We performed Monte Carlo simulations to evaluate the statistical significance of the measured spreads. Most of the GCs in our sample do not show evidence of statistically significant Fe spreads. Only a few exceptions emerge, namely NGC1851, NGC3201, and NGC5634, which display a highly significant iron spread. In particular, NGC3201 shows a particularly pronounced spread in its FG population, reflecting a potential inhomogeneous iron abundance in its pristine material. Finally, through statistical tests, we conclude that our data do not support the presence of a widespread iron variation in GCs.

AI.tldr: This study uses a differential abundance analysis on 92 stars across 13 Galactic GCs to investigate iron spreads. Most GCs show no significant iron variability, with exceptions in NGC1851, NGC3201, and NGC5634. NGC3201 particularly exhibits iron inhomogeneity in its first-generation population. Overall, widespread iron variation in GCs is not supported.
AI.motivation: Recent studies reported significant internal iron spreads in GCs, challenging the traditional view that Fe-peak elements are unaffected in most GCs. This motivates a systematic, precise, and homogeneous investigation to clarify the presence and extent of iron abundance variations among multiple stellar populations in GCs.
AI.method: The authors used the Python-based tool Q2 to determine differential stellar parameters and iron abundances for 92 sibling stars across 13 Galactic GCs. A differential approach was employed to reduce non-LTE effects, observational errors, and systematic biases. Monte Carlo simulations were used to assess the statistical significance of measured iron spreads.
AI.result: Most GCs in the sample show no statistically significant Fe spreads. Three exceptions were found: NGC1851, NGC3201, and NGC5634, which exhibit highly significant iron spreads. NGC3201 shows a particularly pronounced spread in its first-generation (FG) population, indicating potential inhomogeneous iron abundance in its pristine material.
AI.conclusion: Statistical tests indicate that the data do not support widespread iron variation across GCs. The observed iron spreads are limited to a few specific clusters, suggesting that large-scale Fe inhomogeneity is not a common phenomenon in GCs.

title: LISA's view of the Galactic Halo: forecasts for the Galactic double white dwarf population using Gaia data
comment: 12 pages, 2 tables, 5 figures, comments welcome
summary: The population of close double white dwarfs (DWDs) in the Milky Way will make up the largest population of sources resolved by LISA, with a subset of the population having three-dimensional position and chirp mass measurements obtained from LISA observations. Because white dwarfs are the bulk of the Milky Way's stellar remnant population, the positions and masses of close DWDs resolved by LISA are defined by the stellar population properties that host them. Recent Gaia data has unveiled a triaxial accreted component of the Galactic stellar halo: the Gaia-Sausage-Enceladus, which contains stars that are more metal-rich than the extremely metal-poor population of stars residing in the stellar halo beyond 30 kpc. In this work, we assess the size and characteristics of the population of close DWDs using an empirically motivated Galactic model which incorporates the GSE and compare to the classical Galactic model that contains only a single very metal-poor halo population. To do this, we simulate a realistic present-day Galactic DWD population and determine its gravitational wave signal in LISA using LEGWORK. We find that incorporating the metal-rich population from the GSE imprints significant differences in the chirp mass and distance distributions of resolved DWDs, but that the strength and height of the gravitational wave foreground remains unchanged.

AI.tldr: This study models how the Gaia-Sausage-Enceladus (GSE) accreted stellar halo component affects the population of close double white dwarfs (DWDs) detectable by LISA, finding that the GSE's metal-rich population significantly alters chirp mass and distance distributions of resolved DWDs but leaves the gravitational wave foreground unchanged.
AI.motivation: To assess how the recently discovered triaxial accreted component of the Galactic stellar halo (GSE) impacts the predicted population of close DWDs detectable by LISA, compared to classical models that assume only a single very metal-poor halo population.
AI.method: An empirically motivated Galactic model incorporating the GSE was constructed and used to simulate a realistic present-day Galactic DWD population; the gravitational wave signal in LISA was determined using LEGWORK, and results were compared to a classical Galactic model with only a single very metal-poor halo population.
AI.result: Incorporating the GSE's metal-rich population produces significant differences in the chirp mass and distance distributions of resolved DWDs, but does not change the strength and height of the gravitational wave foreground.
AI.conclusion: The GSE accreted component substantially affects the observable properties of resolved DWD populations for LISA, though the unresolved GW foreground remains robust against this updated halo model.

title: Electron Acceleration and Plasma Heating in an Impulsive Confined C-class Solar Flare
summary: A detailed analysis of the impulsive C2.8 solar flare SOL2023-03-19T02:12 is presented, focusing on the microwave (MW) and X-ray domains. The flare was selected because of its impulsive nature, the relatively simple magnetic morphology of its parent active region (AR) NOAA 13256, its confined evolution, its moderate intensity, pronounced non-stationary temporal behaviour, and the availability of a unique multi-wavelength dataset. This dataset includes MW spectral observations in the frequency range 2.8-12 GHz obtained with the new Solar Radio Spectropolarimeter (SOLARSPEL), together with MW images from the Siberian Radioheliograph (SRH). The flare was also observed by two imaging X-ray telescopes, Solar Orbiter/STIX and ASO-S/HXI. Nonlinear force-free field extrapolations are used to reconstruct the three-dimensional magnetic configuration of the AR. We present evidence for a direct coupling between the thermal plasma and the non-thermal electron population in the frame of collisionless plasma during the initial flare stage. Some simple relationships about electron acceleration process are presented and discussed. Analysis of the extrapolated magnetic field indicates that the flare onset was associated with a system of low-lying sheared magnetic loops located along the polarity inversion line (PIL). Given the confined nature of the event and the reconstructed magnetic configuration, we infer that magnetic reconnection most likely occurred within current sheets possessing a substantial guide-field component. The observed non-stationary QPPs in the non-thermal emission, with periods decreasing from approximately 15 to 9 s, are interpreted as signatures of a sequence of magnetic reconnection episodes occurring in different magnetic structures and triggered quasi-periodically by a process that remains uncertain, but which may involve propagating slow magnetoacoustic waves.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the impulsive C2.8 solar flare SOL2023-03-19T02:12 using microwave and X-ray observations, along with magnetic field extrapolations, revealing that the flare onset involved low-lying sheared magnetic loops, magnetic reconnection with a strong guide field, and quasi-periodic pulsations (QPPs) with periods decreasing from ~15 to 9 s, likely caused by sequential reconnection episodes possibly triggered by slow magnetoacoustic waves.
AI.motivation: To understand the coupling between thermal plasma and non-thermal electron populations in collisionless plasma during the impulsive phase of a solar flare, and to characterize the magnetic reconnection processes and quasi-periodic pulsations using a unique multi-wavelength dataset.
AI.method: Multi-wavelength analysis combining MW spectral data (SOLARSPEL, 2.8–12 GHz), MW imaging (SRH), X-ray imaging (Solar Orbiter/STIX, ASO-S/HXI), and nonlinear force-free field (NLFFF) extrapolations to reconstruct the 3D magnetic configuration of AR NOAA 13256.
AI.result: Evidence of direct thermal–non-thermal electron coupling in collisionless plasma during the initial flare stage; flare onset linked to low-lying sheared loops along the PIL; magnetic reconnection occurred in current sheets with a substantial guide-field component; QPPs in non-thermal emission show decreasing periods from ~15 to 9 s, interpreted as sequential reconnection episodes.
AI.conclusion: The confined C2.8 flare arose from sheared magnetic structures with guide-field-dominated reconnection, and the observed QPPs suggest a quasi-periodic triggering mechanism, possibly involving propagating slow magnetoacoustic waves, though the exact process remains uncertain.

title: Effect of Neutron Star Jets on Common Envelope Evolution
comment: 11 pages, 8 figures, 1 table, submitted to ApJ
summary: The common envelope (CE) phase is a key stage in binary star evolution that is still not very well understood. Once engulfed by the giant star, the binary companion may accrete envelope material. For neutron star (NS) companions, such accretion may in principle occur at mass rates several orders of magnitude above the Eddington limit and may result in outflows dominated by powerful bi-polar jets with mass-loss rates similar to the accretion rates. Such jets would impact the morphology of the system and the rate of envelope unbinding, which affect the duration and outcome of the CE event. Employing 3D global hydrodynamic simulations, we study the role of such NS jets in a CE event involving a red giant branch star. The jets eventually drill through and break out of the envelope, producing prominent low-density bi-polar lobes. The jets cause about twice as much envelope mass to be unbound as compared to simulations of the same duration without NS jets. However, the rate of mass unbinding due to the jets decreases towards the ends of the simulations as the jets break out and energetically decouple from the envelope. Moreover, jet activity leads to slightly reduced drag on the binary, decreasing the rate of orbital energy transfer to the envelope. Hence, while such powerful jets can play an important role, negative feedback effects tend to prevent them from dominating envelope unbinding and dictating CE outcomes.

AI.tldr: 3D hydrodynamic simulations show that bipolar jets from a neutron star accreting during a common envelope phase can drill through the envelope, double the amount of unbound mass, but eventually decouple energetically and reduce orbital energy transfer, limiting their overall impact on envelope unbinding and CE outcomes.
AI.motivation: To understand the role of neutron star jets in common envelope evolution, as accretion onto NS companions may occur at super-Eddington rates, potentially driving powerful outflows that could significantly affect envelope unbinding and the morphology of the system.
AI.method: 3D global hydrodynamic simulations of a common envelope event involving a red giant branch star and a neutron star companion, modeling jet feedback from super-Eddington accretion.
AI.result: Jets drill through and break out of the envelope, creating low-density bipolar lobes and unbinding about twice as much envelope mass compared to simulations without jets. However, jet-induced mass unbinding slows as jets break out and decouple from the envelope, and jet activity slightly reduces drag and orbital energy transfer.
AI.conclusion: Powerful NS jets can play an important role in CE events, but negative feedback effects (energetic decoupling, reduced orbital energy transfer) prevent them from dominating envelope unbinding or dictating CE outcomes.

title: Explosive Molecular Outflows
comment: Invited review article for "The Astronomy and Astrophysics Review"
summary: About fifteen years ago a new type of extreme (with kinetic energies of $\mathrm{E}_k \sim 10^{47-49}\, \mathrm{erg}$) molecular outflows associated with very luminous ($\geq 10^5\, \mathrm{L}_\odot$) and massive (10$^3$ M$_\odot$) young clusters was confirmed, the Explosive Molecular Outflows. This new class of outflows is largely different from the classical bipolar protostellar flows, with spatial distributions made of numerous narrow straight filament- or streamer-like ejections in an almost isotropic arrangement and with clear Hubble--Lemaître-like expansion motions. Straight filaments point directly to the center of expanding molecular or ionized shells, which exhibit expansion velocities of about 10--50 km s$^{-1}$. However, no young massive stars are clearly located there, probably because they moved to other places. These physical characteristics suggest that explosive outflows are short-lived in nature and possibly generated by an energetic single and brief disrupting event. The most up-to-date theoretical model for explaining their nature involves the disruption of non-hierarchical massive protostellar systems, where members may either form a close binary (with separations of a few au) or merge into a single massive star, as recently proposed for the nearest high-mass star-forming region, Orion BN/KL.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews Explosive Molecular Outflows (EMOs), a class of extreme molecular outflows discovered ~15 years ago, characterized by isotropic filamentary ejections with Hubble-Lemaître-like expansion, associated with luminous massive young clusters but lacking central massive stars. The leading theoretical model involves disruption of non-hierarchical massive protostellar systems via close binary formation or merger events.
AI.motivation: To characterize and understand a newly confirmed class of extreme molecular outflows (Explosive Molecular Outflows) that differ fundamentally from classical bipolar protostellar outflows, and to provide a theoretical framework for their origin.
AI.method: Review of observational characteristics (kinetic energies ~10^47-49 erg, isotropic filamentary structures, Hubble-Lemaître-like expansion velocities of 10-50 km/s) and comparison with theoretical models involving disruption of non-hierarchical massive protostellar systems.
AI.result: Explosive Molecular Outflows are short-lived, exhibit isotropic distributions of narrow straight filaments with Hubble-Lemaître-like expansion, lack central young massive stars, and are best explained by energetic single disrupting events in non-hierarchical massive protostellar systems (close binary formation or merger).
AI.conclusion: Explosive Molecular Outflows represent a distinct class of outflows likely produced by brief, energetic disruption events in massive protostellar systems, with the Orion BN/KL region as the nearest example.

title: Nonlinear MESA-RSP Modeling of Delta Cephei: Period Matching, Amplitude Control, and Model-Acceptance Diagnostics
comment: 20 pages, 7 figures, 5 tables
summary: Delta Cephei is the prototype classical Cepheid and a useful target for testing whether a one-dimensional nonlinear radial-pulsation workflow can satisfy more than a period constraint. We present a target-specific, reproducible workflow using the Modules for Experiments in Stellar Astrophysics (MESA) Radial Stellar Pulsation (RSP) capability, hereafter MESA-RSP, to construct, tune, extend, and classify nonlinear radial-pulsation calculations for Delta Cephei. A tuned 200-period calculation with M = 5.0 solar masses, Teff = 6050 K, L = 2422.5 solar luminosities, X = 0.73, and Z = 0.007 gives P_model = 5.366622 d for the adopted target P_obs = 5.366531 d, a difference of 9.1 x 10^-5 d, or approximately 7.9 s. This agreement shows that the mean-density structure can be tuned successfully, but extended calculations demonstrate that period matching alone is not an adequate nonlinear model-acceptance criterion. When the period-matched model was continued with the default/weak-damping setup, the amplitude and cycle behavior became nonstationary. We therefore explored the MESA-RSP eddy-viscous damping parameter RSP_alfam as a first amplitude-control experiment. The RSP_alfam = 0.60 case is retained as a period-stable reference, while the RSP_alfam = 0.425, 500-period calculation is classified as the clean amplitude-enhanced candidate. A 700-period continuation is retained only as a diagnostic case because it produced an energy-error warning. The main contribution is a reproducible Delta Cephei workflow and a transparent classification scheme showing that acceptable nonlinear Cepheid models require period agreement, interpretable late-cycle amplitude behavior, controlled surface-velocity diagnostics, and the absence of serious numerical warnings.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a reproducible MESA-RSP workflow for Delta Cephei that achieves period matching to ~7.9 seconds, but demonstrates that period agreement alone is insufficient for nonlinear model acceptance, requiring additional criteria including amplitude stability, surface-velocity diagnostics, and absence of numerical warnings.
AI.motivation: To test whether a one-dimensional nonlinear radial-pulsation workflow can satisfy more than just a period constraint, using Delta Cephei as a prototype target.
AI.method: Used MESA-RSP to construct, tune, extend, and classify nonlinear radial-pulsation calculations for Delta Cephei, exploring the eddy-viscous damping parameter RSP_alfam as an amplitude-control mechanism.
AI.result: Achieved period matching within 7.9 seconds (P_model=5.366622 d vs P_obs=5.366531 d) with M=5.0 Msun, Teff=6050 K, L=2422.5 Lsun, X=0.73, Z=0.007. Found that period-matched models can exhibit nonstationary amplitude/cycle behavior with default damping, leading to classification of models with different RSP_alfam values.
AI.conclusion: Acceptable nonlinear Cepheid models require period agreement, interpretable late-cycle amplitude behavior, controlled surface-velocity diagnostics, and absence of serious numerical warnings—period matching alone is insufficient.

title: Synthetic Observed-Band Light Curves of Delta Cephei from MESA-RSP Models
comment: 16 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables
summary: Observed-band light curves provide a stronger test of Cepheid pulsation models than period matching alone, because the measured photometric amplitude depends on the phase-dependent luminosity, temperature, radius, and passband transformation. We construct synthetic observed-band light curves for delta Cephei from nonlinear radial pulsation models computed with MESA-RSP and transform the model outputs L(phi), Teff(phi), and R(phi) into bolometric magnitudes and MIST bolometric-correction magnitudes in Bessell and Gaia passbands. The adopted AAVSO Johnson V-band template has peak-to-peak amplitude Delta V_obs = 0.8390 mag. The period-stable reference model with RSP alfam = 0.60 gives a MIST-BC V-band amplitude of only Delta V_syn approximately 0.0106 mag. Amplitude-enhanced models increase the synthetic amplitude, with RSP alfam = 0.425 giving 0.0302 mag, RSP alfam = 0.400 giving 0.0344 mag, and the accepted RSP alfam = 0.400, RSP alfat = 0.095 model giving 0.0367 mag. The final accepted model therefore reaches only 4.4 percent of the observed Johnson V-band amplitude. These results show that MIST-BC transformations are necessary for a physically meaningful observed-band comparison, but they do not remove the amplitude discrepancy in the present model sequence. The remaining mismatch indicates that the dominant limitation is the small nonlinear pulsation amplitude of the models rather than the bolometric-correction transformation itself.

AI.tldr: Cepheid pulsation models from MESA-RSP produce synthetic light curves that match only 4.4% of the observed Johnson V-band amplitude for δ Cephei, indicating the primary limitation is the small nonlinear pulsation amplitude of the models rather than the bolometric-correction transformations.
AI.motivation: Observed-band light curves provide a stronger test of Cepheid pulsation models than period matching alone, because the measured photometric amplitude depends on phase-dependent luminosity, temperature, radius, and passband transformation.
AI.method: Construct synthetic observed-band light curves for δ Cephei from nonlinear radial pulsation models computed with MESA-RSP; transform model outputs L(φ), T_eff(φ), and R(φ) into bolometric magnitudes and MIST bolometric-correction magnitudes in Bessell and Gaia passbands.
AI.result: The period-stable reference model gives ΔV_syn ≈ 0.0106 mag. Amplitude-enhanced models yield up to 0.0367 mag, reaching only 4.4% of the observed Johnson V-band amplitude (ΔV_obs = 0.8390 mag).
AI.conclusion: MIST-BC transformations are necessary for physically meaningful observed-band comparison but do not resolve the amplitude discrepancy; the dominant limitation is the small nonlinear pulsation amplitude of the models rather than the bolometric-correction transformation.

title: Radial Evolution of Near-Sun Magnetic Switchbacks Alfvenicity, Occurrence Rate, and Size
comment: 21 pages, 10 figures
summary: Magnetic switchbacks, characterized by reversals of magnetic field direction, are widely observed in the inner heliosphere by Parker Solar Probe (PSP). With PSP reaching perihelia near 10Rs, observations from the first 24 encounters enable studies of near-Sun switchback evolution at r > 10Rs. We construct a switchback catalog within 10 < r < 55Rs by identifying magnetic field reversals with stable field magnitude and strahl-electron polarity. Statistical analysis shows that switchback Alfvenicity decreases with increasing radial distance, consistent with solar wind evolution beyond the Alfven critical point. Meanwhile, switchback occurrence rate and spatial size increase with distance, suggesting continued generation and expansion during solar wind propagation. At a given radial distance, the fraction of solar wind containing switchbacks is positively correlated with background solar wind radial velocity (VR) and Alfven Mach number (MA), while the local occurrence rate is mainly controlled by MA. These results suggest that switchback patches preferentially form in faster and higher-MA solar wind. The spatial size of switchbacks shows no clear dependence on MA or VR, implying that their size evolution is probably not determined by source conditions. Solar activity influences switchback evolution through changes in background solar wind properties, with a larger fraction of higher-MA switchbacks during solar minimum. We further identify anisotropy relative to the background magnetic field direction: the local occurrence rate and spatial size are approximately 1.5 times as large in the perpendicular direction as in the parallel direction, indicating distinct magnetic topology of switchback patches

AI.tldr: Analysis of PSP data (first 24 encounters, r = 10-55 Rs) shows switchback Alfvenicity decreases with distance, while occurrence rate and size increase. Switchbacks preferentially form in faster, higher-MA solar wind. Spatial size shows no clear dependence on source conditions. Local occurrence rate is controlled by MA, and anisotropy reveals ~1.5x larger occurrence/size perpendicular to the background magnetic field.
AI.motivation: To understand the evolution, generation, and properties of magnetic switchbacks in the inner heliosphere near the Sun (r > 10 Rs) using PSP observations from the first 24 encounters.
AI.method: Constructed a switchback catalog within 10 < r < 55 Rs by identifying magnetic field reversals with stable field magnitude and strahl-electron polarity. Statistical analysis of radial evolution, dependence on solar wind speed (VR), Alfven Mach number (MA), solar activity, and magnetic field topology.
AI.result: Switchback Alfvenicity decreases with radial distance; occurrence rate and spatial size increase with distance. Switchback fraction correlates positively with VR and MA; local occurrence rate controlled mainly by MA. Spatial size independent of MA or VR. Anisotropy: occurrence rate and size ~1.5x larger perpendicular to background B-field.
AI.conclusion: Switchback patches preferentially form in faster, higher-MA solar wind. Size evolution likely not determined by source conditions. Solar activity influences via background solar wind properties. Switchback patches have distinct magnetic topology with perpendicular anisotropy.

title: Nucleosynthesis of Pop III and Fe-enriched Pop II Pair-Instability Supernovae
comment: 26 pages, 25 figures
summary: Recently discovered very metal-poor (VMP) star LAMOST J1010+2358 shows a peculiar abundance pattern that is remarkably well fit by a Pop III pair-instability supernova (PISN) of $\simeq 260$ M$_\odot$. Motivated by this, we investigate the nucleosynthetic characteristics of Pop III and Pop II PISNe to provide theoretical constraints for future observations. This paper is divided into two parts. First, we explore the evolution and nucleosynthesis of Pop III PISNe with initial masses of 130 - 300 M$_\odot$. Our main aim is to investigate how the uncertainty in $^{12}$C$(α,γ)^{16}$O and $^{16}$O+$^{16}$O reaction rates affect their explosion properties and nucleosynthesis. We find that the yields of odd-$Z$ elements are particularly sensitive to the $^{12}$C$(α,γ)^{16}$O rate, while the production of Fe-peak elements shows significant sensitivity to both rates. Second, we investigate the nucleosynthetic features of Pop II PISNe formed in gas enriched exclusively by Pop III PISN ejecta. By employing a time-dependent convection model during the explosion, we demonstrate that metal enrichment increases opacity and triggers vigorous convective mixing. This hydrodynamic effect significantly enhances the explosion energy and $^{56}\text{Ni}$ production. Consequently, Pop II PISNe exhibit distinct chemical signatures, including a weaker odd-even effect and enhanced Zn-Ge production, providing unique diagnostics for identifying PISN remnants in the early Universe.

AI.tldr: This paper examines nucleosynthetic characteristics of Pop III and Pop II pair-instability supernovae (PISNe), focusing on how nuclear reaction rate uncertainties affect yields and how metal enrichment alters explosion dynamics and chemical signatures.
AI.motivation: The recent discovery of VMP star LAMOST J1010+2358 with an abundance pattern fitting a ~260 M☉ Pop III PISN motivates the need for theoretical constraints on PISN nucleosynthesis to guide future observations.
AI.method: Two-part study: (1) Evolution and nucleosynthesis of Pop III PISNe (130-300 M☉) varying the ¹²C(α,γ)¹⁶O and ¹⁶O+¹⁶O reaction rates; (2) Pop II PISNe formed in gas enriched by Pop III PISN ejecta using a time-dependent convection model.
AI.result: Odd-Z element yields are highly sensitive to the ¹²C(α,γ)¹⁶O rate, while Fe-peak elements are sensitive to both rates. Metal enrichment increases opacity, triggering vigorous convective mixing that boosts explosion energy and ⁵⁶Ni production in Pop II PISNe.
AI.conclusion: Pop II PISNe exhibit distinct chemical signatures (weaker odd-even effect, enhanced Zn-Ge production) that serve as unique diagnostics for identifying PISN remnants in the early Universe.

title: SN 2025aico: Early observations of a faint Type IIb supernova with a low-mass envelope
comment: 20 pages, 14 figures, submit to Astronomy & Astrophysics
summary: We aimed to investigate the physical properties and the underlying explosion mechanisms of the Type IIb SN 2025aico. Through a comprehensive analysis of early-phase optical light curves and spectroscopic data, we aim to constrain the fundamental explosion parameters and evaluate the physical state of the event. We present early multi-band optical imaging and low-resolution optical spectroscopic follow-up observations of the Type IIb SN 2025aico, spanning approximately 70 days from the explosion. We constrain the properties of SN 2025aico by utilizing a hybrid model that combines shock-cooling emission and radioactively powered diffusion, as well as by analyzing the spectroscopic evolution. We use various approaches to constrain the 56Ni mixing from early data, and also compared our spectra with models to constrain the properties of the progenitor. The explosion epoch of SN 2025aico is estimated to be MJD 61032.69, while the rise time in the r_M-band is 22.30 +/- 0.70 days. The peak pseudo-bolometric luminosity in the optical bands is L_opt = (4.07 +/- 0.10) x 10^41 erg/s. The fitting yields a moderate to relatively low 56Ni mass of M_Ni = 0.033 +0.006/-0.004 M_sun and an ejecta mass of M_ej = 2.79 +0.21/-0.18 M_sun. The photospheric velocity near the bolometric peak, measured from the Fe II lambda 5169 line, is 6450 +180/-160 km/s. The derived envelope properties suggest a compact He-star progenitor possessing an H-rich envelope of M_env approx. 0.01 M_sun and a radius of R_env approx. 6-10 R_sun. The derived physical properties of SN 2025aico indicate an origin from a moderate-mass, stripped He-star in a compact binary system, characterized by a minimal residual hydrogen envelope. The explosion itself demonstrates weak to moderate 56Ni mixing throughout the ejecta.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the Type IIb supernova SN 2025aico through early optical light curves and spectroscopy over ~70 days. The explosion epoch, rise time, peak luminosity, 56Ni mass (0.033 M☉), ejecta mass (2.79 M☉), and photospheric velocity (6450 km/s) are derived. The progenitor is a compact He-star with a minimal H-rich envelope (≈0.01 M☉, radius ≈6-10 R☉) in a compact binary system, exhibiting weak-to-moderate 56Ni mixing.
AI.motivation: To investigate the physical properties and explosion mechanisms of the Type IIb SN 2025aico by constraining its fundamental explosion parameters and progenitor state.
AI.method: Multi-band optical imaging and low-resolution optical spectroscopy over ~70 days; hybrid model combining shock-cooling emission and radioactively powered diffusion; spectroscopic evolution analysis; comparison with models to constrain progenitor properties.
AI.result: Explosion epoch: MJD 61032.69; rise time in r_M-band: 22.30±0.70 days; peak pseudo-bolometric luminosity: (4.07±0.10)×10^41 erg/s; 56Ni mass: 0.033+0.006/−0.004 M☉; ejecta mass: 2.79+0.21/−0.18 M☉; photospheric velocity (Fe II λ5169): 6450+180/−160 km/s; H-rich envelope mass: ≈0.01 M☉; envelope radius: ≈6-10 R☉.
AI.conclusion: SN 2025aico originated from a moderate-mass, stripped He-star in a compact binary system with a minimal residual hydrogen envelope. The explosion shows weak-to-moderate 56Ni mixing throughout the ejecta.

title: Rediscussion of eclipsing binaries. Paper 32. The eccentric F-type system EY Cephei
comment: Accepted for publication in The Observatory. 13 pages, 5 tables, 5 colour figures
summary: EY Cep is a detached eclipsing binary containing two F0 V stars in an orbit with a period of 7.97 d and an eccentricity of 0.440. We determine the physical properties of the system using light-curves from the Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite and published spectroscopic measurements. We find masses of 1.523 +/- 0.008 Msun and 1.494 +/- 0.014 Msun, radii of 1.491 +/- 0.004 Rsun and 1.446 +/- 0.004 Rsun, and temperatures of 7070 +/- 170 K and 6990 +/- 150 K. We calculate the system's distance to be 300.3 +/- 3.8 pc, in excellent agreement with the Gaia DR3 parallax, and estimate its age to be 220 Myr. We find no evidence for pulsations, but there are hints of variation in the eclipse times potentially attributable to a third body on a longer-period eccentric orbit.

AI.tldr: EY Cep is a detached eclipsing binary (F0 V stars, 7.97 d period, e=0.440). Using TESS light-curves and published spectroscopy, we determine precise masses (1.523, 1.494 Msun), radii, temperatures, distance (300.3 pc, agreeing with Gaia DR3), and age (220 Myr). No pulsations detected; eclipse timing variations hint at a possible third body on a long-period eccentric orbit.
AI.motivation: To accurately determine the physical properties (masses, radii, temperatures, distance, age) of the detached eclipsing binary EY Cep using high-quality TESS photometry combined with published spectroscopic data.
AI.method: Analysis of TESS light-curves and published spectroscopic radial-velocity measurements to derive binary parameters via light-curve and orbit fitting.
AI.result: EY Cep masses: 1.523±0.008 Msun and 1.494±0.014 Msun; radii: 1.491±0.004 Rsun and 1.446±0.004 Rsun; temperatures: 7070±170 K and 6990±150 K. Distance = 300.3±3.8 pc (agrees with Gaia DR3). Age ≈ 220 Myr. No pulsations detected; possible eclipse timing variations hint at a third body on a longer-period eccentric orbit.
AI.conclusion: EY Cep's fundamental stellar parameters are precisely measured, consistent with Gaia astrometry, and there is tentative evidence for a third companion from eclipse timing variations.

title: Asteroseismic forward modelling of 36 $β$ Cep pulsators and inferences on their internal differential rotation
comment: Submitted to Astronomy and Astrophysics
summary: Asteroseismic observations of the interior rotation of main sequence stars have shown that angular momentum transport is much more efficient than expected. Which transport mechanisms are responsible for this is still unclear. Detections of radial differential rotation provide valuable constraints on these transport mechanisms. This has been detected in several massive main sequence $β$ Cep pulsators, even though fewer than ten $β$ Cep stars have been asteroseismically modelled in detail so far. We aim to expand the sample of asteroseismically forward modelled $β$ Cep pulsators to maximally exploit their potential to observationally constrain angular momentum transport mechanisms. To that end, we seek to constrain their rotation profiles. We searched for rotational splitting of non-radial modes in a large $β$ Cep sample with identified mode degrees. These were subjected to a novel forward modelling approach, which consistently accounts for second-order rotation effects using the state-of-the-art StORM oscillation code. We successfully modelled 36 $β$ Cep stars and constrained crucial parameters such as their initial mass, internal rotation frequency, convective core mass, and age. Like in intermediate-mass main sequence stars, the internal rotation rate globally decreases in $β$ Cep stars as they evolve along the main sequence. Radial differential rotation is constrained in 17 $β$ Cep stars. The rotation rate in at least 14 stars varies by more than 10%. Of these 14 stars, ten have their rotation rate decreasing from the core to the surface while it is the opposite in four of them. We affirm that radial differential rotation is common in $β$ Cep stars. Moreover, our constrained rotation profiles suggest that the typical $β$ Cep rotation profile may be non-monotonic.

AI.tldr: This study modeled 36 β Cep stars using asteroseismology to constrain internal rotation profiles, finding radial differential rotation common in 17 stars, with rotation generally decreasing from core to surface in most but increasing in some, and that internal rotation globally decreases as stars evolve along the main sequence.
AI.motivation: Angular momentum transport in main sequence stars is more efficient than predicted by theory, and the responsible transport mechanisms remain unclear. Detections of radial differential rotation in massive β Cep pulsators provide valuable observational constraints, but fewer than ten such stars had been asteroseismically modeled in detail, motivating an expansion of the sample.
AI.method: The authors searched for rotational splitting of non-radial modes in a large sample of β Cep stars with identified mode degrees, then applied a novel forward modeling approach using the StORM oscillation code to consistently account for second-order rotation effects.
AI.result: Successfully modeled 36 β Cep stars, constraining initial mass, internal rotation frequency, convective core mass, and age. Radial differential rotation was constrained in 17 stars, with 14 showing >10% variation (10 with rotation decreasing from core to surface, 4 with the opposite trend). Internal rotation rate globally decreases along the main sequence.
AI.conclusion: Radial differential rotation is common in β Cep stars, and the typical rotation profile may be non-monotonic, providing important constraints on angular momentum transport mechanisms in massive stars.

title: Investigating the electron temperature of [Ar IV] in planetary nebulae using the DESIRED database
comment: 12 pages, 8 figures, 3 tables. Accepted for publication in Astronomy & Astrophysics
summary: (Abridged) We investigate the behaviour of the electron temperature derived from [Ar IV] lines, Te([Ar IV]), in a sample of PNe to characterize the thermal structure of high-excitation gas and compare it with the predictions of standard photoionisation models. Using the DEep Spectra of ionised REgions Data Base Extended (DESIRED-E), we selected a sample of 57 PNe for which Te([Ar IV]), Te([O III]), and ne([Ar IV]) could be determined simultaneously and homogeneously. We performed a detailed comparison between these observational diagnostics and a suite of over 160000 photoionisation models from the Mexican Million Models Database (3MdB). We find that the observed Te([Ar IV]) values are systematically higher than those predicted by pure photoionisation models for a given Te([O III]), i.e. approximately 31 percent of the PNe sample exhibits a Te([Ar IV]) more than 2sigma higher than photoionization model predictions. This discrepancy persists regardless of the specific set of auroral lines used for the diagnostic or the choice of atomic data (transition probabilities and collision strengths) adopted in the calculations. Other high-ionisation Te diagnostics, compiled from the DESIRED database or from the literature, however, do not show such behaviour, though the statistics for these are much more limited. The fact that models succeed for the highest-ionisation species but fail specifically for [Ar IV] suggests that the discrepancy is not due to a global inner-nebula heating mechanism. Instead, it points toward a localized physical effect or a limitation in the current understanding of the ionisation stratification and atomic physics specific to the Argon ion stages. This "[Ar IV] anomaly" must be resolved to ensure the reliability of abundance determinations in high-excitation nebulae.

AI.tldr: The electron temperature derived from [Ar IV] lines in planetary nebulae is systematically higher (~31% of a 57-PN sample exceeds 2σ) than predictions from over 160,000 photoionisation models, while other high-ionisation diagnostics match models. This "[Ar IV] anomaly" points to a localised physical effect or atomic physics limitation, not a global heating mechanism.
AI.motivation: To characterize the thermal structure of high-excitation gas in planetary nebulae and test whether standard photoionisation models can reproduce observed [Ar IV] electron temperatures.
AI.method: Selected 57 PNe from the DESIRED-E database with simultaneous, homogeneous Te([Ar IV]), Te([O III]), and ne([Ar IV]) measurements; compared observations against >160,000 photoionisation models from the 3MdB database.
AI.result: Te([Ar IV]) is systematically higher than model predictions given Te([O III]); ~31% of the sample exceeds 2σ discrepancy. The anomaly persists across different auroral lines and atomic data sets. Other high-ionisation diagnostics do not show this behaviour.
AI.conclusion: The [Ar IV] anomaly is not caused by a global inner-nebula heating mechanism but likely by a localised physical effect or a limitation in the ionisation stratification and atomic physics of argon. Resolving this is essential for reliable abundance determinations in high-excitation nebulae.

title: What Causes the Asymmetry of Conjugate Hard X-Ray Footpoints in Solar Flares?
comment: 20 pages, 7 figures, 1 table
summary: Hard X-ray (HXR) emission in solar flares critically diagnoses nonthermal electron acceleration, transport, and precipitation. Observations commonly show asymmetric HXR photon fluxes between paired footpoints, whose physical origin remains debated, as the conventional magnetic mirroring mechanism often fails to explain the observed asymmetry. Here we performed rigorous statistical tests on the association between photospheric magnetic parameters within the HXR footpoint regions and the asymmetry of HXR production. We analyzed 103 time intervals taken from around the peaks of HXR bursts in 67 M- and X-class flares with a clear double-ribbon morphology observed by both the Ramaty High Energy Solar Spectroscopic Imager and the Solar Dynamics Observatory. We found that conjugate HXR footpoint sources are asymmetric in photon fluxes, maximum intensities, and sizes, but rather symmetric in mean intensities. The photon flux ratio of the stronger over weaker footpoint source shows a strong linear correlation with the size ratio and a nonlinear correlation with the maximum intensity ratio. The asymmetry of magnetic field strength and flux at the conjugate footpoints shows a positive correlation with the HXR asymmetry, contrary to what magnetic mirroring effects predict. Importantly, the asymmetry of unsigned photospheric vertical electric current (PVEC) exhibits a strong positive correlation with the HXR footpoint asymmetry. PVEC at HXR footpoints most likely maps the footprints of coronal current layers where flaring reconnections occur. This tight linkage suggests that the electric-current-associated physical processes, including reconnection-induced electric fields and current-driven micro-turbulence, are at work to modulate the production and precipitation of nonthermal electrons.

AI.tldr: Statistical analysis of 103 HXR burst intervals in 67 solar flares reveals that HXR footpoint asymmetries correlate strongly with unsigned photospheric vertical electric current (PVEC) asymmetry, not with magnetic mirroring predictions, suggesting electric-current-associated processes modulate nonthermal electron production and precipitation.
AI.motivation: To resolve the long-standing debate on the physical origin of asymmetric hard X-ray photon fluxes between paired footpoints in solar flares, given that the conventional magnetic mirroring mechanism often fails to explain observed asymmetries.
AI.method: Performed rigorous statistical tests on the association between photospheric magnetic parameters and HXR asymmetry, analyzing 103 time intervals around peaks of HXR bursts in 67 M- and X-class flares with clear double-ribbon morphology, using data from RHESSI and SDO.
AI.result: Conjugate HXR footpoints are asymmetric in photon fluxes, maximum intensities, and sizes, but symmetric in mean intensities. HXR asymmetry shows strong linear correlation with size ratio and nonlinear correlation with maximum intensity ratio. Magnetic field asymmetry positively correlates with HXR asymmetry (opposite to mirroring predictions). PVEC asymmetry exhibits a strong positive correlation with HXR footpoint asymmetry.
AI.conclusion: The tight linkage between PVEC and HXR asymmetry suggests electric-current-associated physical processes (reconnection-induced electric fields and current-driven micro-turbulence) modulate nonthermal electron production and precipitation, rather than magnetic mirroring effects.

title: Magnetic field configuration favoring X-class solar flares: violation of Joy's and Hale's laws
comment: 13 pages, 5 figures, 1 table
summary: We investigate the statistical relationship between magnetic tilt angle and X-class flare productivity using 39 flare-productive active regions that produced 64 X-class flares. By classifying global magnetic tilt properties into four quadrants based on compliance with Hale's and Joy's laws, we identify their distinctive flare productivity characteristics. The Green quadrant (following both laws) contains 18 active regions producing 24 X-class flares, while the Yellow quadrant (following Hale's law but violating Joy's law) shows significantly enhanced productivity with 18 active regions producing 36 X-class flares. The Black quadrant (violating both laws), though containing only 3 active regions, produces 4 X-class flares. More importantly, flares in both the Yellow and Black quadrants exhibit systematically higher flare classes compared to those in the Green quadrant. Our statistical analysis demonstrates that violation of Hale's or Joy's law at the global scale are strongly associated with increased flare occurrence and flare intensity. Furthermore, examination of globally normal (Green quadrant) regions reveals that localized tilt anomalies are universally present and also contribute to X-class flare production. These results establish that abnormal magnetic tilt configurations - whether occurring at the global or local scale - are key indicators of major flare activity.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 39 active regions producing 64 X-class flares shows that violations of Hale's and Joy's laws in magnetic tilt angles are strongly associated with increased X-class flare productivity and intensity, establishing abnormal magnetic tilt as a key indicator of major flare activity.
AI.motivation: To investigate the statistical relationship between magnetic tilt angle properties (compliance with Hale's and Joy's laws) and X-class flare productivity across a substantial sample of flare-productive active regions.
AI.method: Classified 39 flare-productive active regions (producing 64 X-class flares) into four quadrants based on global magnetic tilt compliance with Hale's and Joy's laws: Green (follows both), Yellow (follows Hale's but violates Joy's), and Black (violates both), then compared flare productivity and intensity across categories, and examined localized tilt anomalies in normal regions.
AI.result: Green quadrant: 18 ARs, 24 X-flares; Yellow quadrant: 18 ARs, 36 X-flares (significantly enhanced); Black quadrant: 3 ARs, 4 X-flares. Yellow and Black quadrants show systematically higher flare classes. Localized tilt anomalies present in all normal regions also contribute to X-class flares.
AI.conclusion: Violations of Hale's or Joy's law at global scale are strongly associated with increased flare occurrence and intensity; abnormal magnetic tilt configurations (global or local) are key indicators of major flare activity.

title: The white dwarf population of open clusters and their tidal tails. Tracers of contamination and stellar interactions
comment: 12 pages main text, 48 pages appendix. Appendix C is only available on arXiv. Tables B1 and B2 will be available on CDS. Accepted in A&A
summary: Recent Gaia studies have identified numerous open clusters (OCs) & tidal tail catalogues, enabling systematic searches for white dwarfs (WDs) associated with clusters & their extended structures. We compile a literature-based sample of OC-WD pairs to validate WD membership in cluster cores & tails, investigate the initial-final mass relation (IFMR), identify WDs formed through non-canonical evolution, and interpret the observed WD populations using a grid of N-body simulations. We combine Gaia DR3 cluster & tail catalogues with UV-IR photometry to analyse the OC-WD pairs. WD masses, cooling ages, radii, temperatures & luminosities are estimated using colour-magnitude diagrams & spectral energy distributions. These observations are interpreted in the context of N-body simulations. We identify 235 OC-WD pairs (99 in tails) in 80 clusters. More than 28% of the pairs are likely spurious, with contamination substantially higher in the tails (>48%) than in the cluster cores (>13%), indicating significant field-star contamination in current Gaia-based catalogues. The Pleiade tails also show severe contamination by old WDs. Simulations predict that the fraction of core WDs increases with cluster age, reaching >10%, whereas the observed fractions remain systematically lower, consistent with the WD deficit problem. Despite the high contamination rate, most tail WDs (~83%) are consistent with having been born inside the tidal radius. We also identify 63 candidate binary-origin WDs & 47 new IFMR candidates. WDs provide a powerful probe of contamination in cluster and tail catalogues and place important constraints on cluster detection methods & N-body simulations. Resolving the WD deficit and improving membership validation will require improved observations, membership methods, WD physics, and spectroscopic follow-up, enabling stronger constraints on dynamical cluster evolution & the WD IFMR.

AI.tldr: This paper compiles 235 open cluster-white dwarf pairs (99 in tails) from 80 clusters using Gaia DR3 and UV-IR photometry, finding >28% contamination (tails >48%, cores >13%) and systematically lower observed WD fractions than N-body simulations predict, consistent with the white dwarf deficit problem. It identifies 63 candidate binary-origin WDs and 47 new IFMR candidates.
AI.motivation: To validate white dwarf membership in open cluster cores and tidal tails, investigate the initial-final mass relation, identify non-canonically evolved WDs, and interpret observed WD populations using N-body simulations, while also probing contamination in Gaia-based cluster and tail catalogues.
AI.method: Combined Gaia DR3 cluster and tail catalogues with UV-IR photometry; estimated WD masses, cooling ages, radii, temperatures, and luminosities using colour-magnitude diagrams and spectral energy distributions; interpreted observations with a grid of N-body simulations.
AI.result: Identified 235 OC-WD pairs (99 in tails) across 80 clusters; >28% contamination overall (>48% in tails, >13% in cores); observed WD fractions systematically lower than simulations predict; 63 candidate binary-origin WDs and 47 new IFMR candidates found; ~83% of tail WDs consistent with birth inside tidal radius.
AI.conclusion: WDs are effective probes of contamination in cluster/tail catalogues. Resolving the WD deficit and improving membership requires better observations, membership methods, WD physics, and spectroscopic follow-up to constrain dynamical cluster evolution and the IFMR.

title: Direct detections of white dwarfs in four WD+dM post-common envelope binaries within 20 pc
comment: 11 pages, 7 figures, plus appendix. Published in MNRAS 2026-07-14
summary: Characterising post-common envelope binaries (PCEBs) containing a white dwarf and a main-sequence companion is essential for improving theories of binary evolution. This paper presents the first direct spectroscopic confirmations of the white dwarf components in four PCEB systems within 20 pc of the Sun: G 203-47, GJ 207.1, LHS 1817, and Wolf 1130. To detect the white dwarfs we obtained near-UV spectroscopy from STIS on the Hubble Space Telescope, fitting with white dwarf models and M dwarf proxy spectra. We provide estimates of the white dwarf effective temperatures, which range from approximately 5300 K to 6300 K. We compare these parameters to those determined from modelling with photometry alone, and find a 5 - 8 per cent discrepancy, due to emission features. Notably, 27 years after its initial detection, we confirm the presence of a white dwarf in G 203-47, which is the ninth closest white dwarf to the Sun. Using Swift XRT data, we find that despite the 14.9-day orbital period of G 203-47, it is not tidally locked, possessing a rotation period likely exceeding 100 days, and making it a rare example of a long-period PCEB formed via a brief common envelope interaction. We update the local white dwarf space density to (5.2 $\pm $0.4) $\times$ 10$^{-3}$ pc$^{-3}$, and compare our results to models from the Binary Populations and Spectral Synthesis (BPASS) framework, finding a good agreement with the predicted and observed numbers of PCEBs within 20 pc.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first direct spectroscopic confirmations of white dwarf components in four nearby PCEBs using HST/STIS near-UV spectroscopy, providing effective temperature estimates (5300-6300 K), revealing a 5-8% discrepancy with photometric modeling, confirming G 203-47 as the ninth closest white dwarf, finding it not tidally locked despite a 14.9-day orbit, and updating the local white dwarf space density to (5.2 ± 0.4) × 10⁻³ pc⁻³ with good agreement to BPASS models.
AI.motivation: Characterising PCEBs (white dwarf + main-sequence binaries) is essential for improving theories of binary evolution, particularly the common envelope phase.
AI.method: Near-UV spectroscopy from STIS on the Hubble Space Telescope, fitting with white dwarf model atmospheres and M dwarf proxy spectra; Swift XRT data for rotation period analysis; comparison with BPASS population synthesis models.
AI.result: Confirmed white dwarfs in four PCEBs within 20 pc; effective temperatures ~5300-6300 K; 5-8% discrepancy with photometric-only modeling; G 203-47 is the ninth closest white dwarf and not tidally locked (rotation >100 days despite 14.9-day orbit); updated local WD space density = (5.2 ± 0.4) × 10⁻³ pc⁻³.
AI.conclusion: The results confirm the systems as genuine PCEBs, highlight limitations of photometric-only modeling, identify G 203-47 as a rare long-period PCEB from brief common envelope interaction, and show good agreement between observed PCEB numbers and BPASS population synthesis predictions within 20 pc.

title: Bayesian Inference of Stellar r-Process Abundances
comment: 16 pages, 8 figures
summary: The abundances of heavy elements observed in metal-poor, r-process-enhanced stars provide unique information about the astrophysical conditions in which the rapid neutron-capture process (r-process) occurs. We present a Bayesian framework to infer these conditions from stellar abundance patterns. Building on a large site-independent r-process survey, we use a Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler to fit observed abundances with weighted superpositions of abundances from nucleosynthesis calculations, each parameterized by an initial electron fraction, entropy, and expansion timescale. Applying this framework to the nearly complete r-process template star HD222925, we find that two components are required: A heavier H-component producing elements from the second to the third peak and a lighter L-component producing elements from the first to the second peak. Increasing the number of components does not significantly improve the agreement with observations. Extending the analysis to the limited-r stars HD128279 and HD122563, and to the solar r-process residuals, we find that their abundances can be reproduced by almost the same two components, albeit with different relative weights. Moreover, the lightest neutron-capture elements ($Z \lesssim 35$) in the Sun require additional contributions absent in HD222925. Residual discrepancies are concentrated in elements sensitive to observational systematics (e.g., Ag and Cd) or nuclear-physics inputs (around the third peak). Our study highlights the power of combining stellar abundances with nucleosynthesis calculations to constrain the astrophysical conditions of the r-process, and motivates further improvements in both observational data and nuclear-physics inputs.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a Bayesian MCMC framework to infer r-process astrophysical conditions from stellar abundances. Applied to HD222925, two components (H-component for second-to-third peak, L-component for first-to-second peak) suffice. The same two components, with different weights, also reproduce abundances in limited-r stars and solar r-process residuals, though the Sun needs extra lighter-element contributions. Residual discrepancies trace to observational systematics (Ag, Cd) and nuclear physics (third peak).
AI.motivation: Heavy-element abundances in metal-poor, r-process-enhanced stars carry unique information about the astrophysical conditions of the r-process, motivating a need to quantitatively infer those conditions from observed abundance patterns.
AI.method: A Bayesian framework with MCMC sampling is developed. It fits observed stellar abundances with weighted superpositions of nucleosynthesis calculations parameterized by initial electron fraction, entropy, and expansion timescale, building on a large site-independent r-process survey.
AI.result: Two components (H and L) are sufficient for HD222925; more components do not improve fit. The same two components, with different weights, reproduce abundances in HD128279, HD122563, and solar r-process residuals. The Sun requires extra contributions for Z≲35. Residual discrepancies cluster around elements affected by observational systematics (Ag, Cd) or nuclear-physics inputs (third-peak region).
AI.conclusion: Combining stellar abundances with nucleosynthesis calculations via Bayesian inference powerfully constrains r-process conditions. Further observational and nuclear-physics improvements are needed to resolve remaining discrepancies.

title: Potential Solar Precursors to Magnetic Switchbacks
comment: 86 pages, 20 figrues, accepted for publication in Space Science Reviews
summary: The origin of magnetic switchbacks-large-amplitude, spherically polarized magnetic-field fluctuations with local polarity inversions shown to be nearly ubiquitous in the inner heliosphere by Parker Solar Probe-is presently one of the most outstanding open questions in solar and heliospheric science. The occurrence of these structures in the young solar wind is a topic of active research, focusing not only on their characteristics and evolution but also on unraveling the puzzle of their origin. We first discuss the potential influence on switchback (SB) formation of large-scale coronal dynamics and restructuring processes that shape and regulate the extended solar corona. Following that, we review the dynamics, physical properties, occurrence rate, and energetics of numerous possible precursor small-scale dynamic events that occur throughout the solar atmosphere, emphasizing their possible roles in creating SBs. Finally, we discuss some recent studies attempting to connect in situ observations of SBs and SB patches with remote-sensing observations, and the related challenges in identifying solar wind source regions precisely and linking in situ observations to transient solar phenomena. We also clarify the terminology used by the solar community to describe small-scale solar phenomena.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews the origin of magnetic switchbacks in the solar wind, discussing large-scale coronal dynamics, small-scale precursor events, and challenges in connecting in situ with remote-sensing observations.
AI.motivation: The origin of magnetic switchbacks (large-amplitude, spherically polarized magnetic-field fluctuations with local polarity inversions) in the inner heliosphere is one of the most outstanding open questions in solar and heliospheric science.
AI.method: The paper reviews three areas: (1) large-scale coronal dynamics and restructuring processes, (2) small-scale precursor dynamic events in the solar atmosphere and their possible roles in creating SBs, and (3) attempts to connect in situ SB observations with remote-sensing observations, including clarifying terminology for small-scale solar phenomena.
AI.result: The paper identifies challenges in precisely identifying solar wind source regions and linking in situ observations to transient solar phenomena, while discussing the characteristics, occurrence rate, and energetics of potential precursor events.
AI.conclusion: The origin of magnetic switchbacks remains an open puzzle; connecting in situ and remote-sensing observations is key but faces significant challenges.

title: Pre-flare and active region plasma flows and structure seen by the short wavelength camera on SOLAR-C/EUVST
comment: Published in PASJ
summary: The mechanisms triggering solar flares and driving coronal heating occur across wide temperature ranges on small spatial scales and short timescales, making them difficult to observe with current instrumentation. The upcoming SOLAR-C mission, launching in the late 2020s, will provide unprecedented plasma diagnostic capability with its high-throughput extreme-ultraviolet (EUV) spectroscopic telescope (EUVST), capable of ~0.2 arcsec/pix spatial sampling (~0.4 arcsec resolution), continuous temperature coverage from 0.02-15 MK, and exposure times down to 0.5 seconds. We present forward modelling of the spectrograph's short wavelength camera (170-210 Å; SOLAR-C/EUVST-SW) and its response to log T~6.2 coronal plasma in a three-dimensional MHD-simulated pre-flare active region. We compare this performance to that of the previous-generation EUV Imaging Spectrometer (EIS) on Hinode (SOLAR-B). Our results demonstrate that SOLAR-C/EUVST can distinguish individual flux tubes in simulated active region loops which Hinode/EIS cannot resolve. In simulated pre-flare plasma, SOLAR-C/EUVST captures sharp velocity gradients between adjacent upflowing and downflowing plasma which Hinode/EIS is unable to resolve. Doppler velocity measurement accuracy will reach better than 1 km/s in active regions. We show that this next-generation spectrograph can be expected to directly observe processes potentially related to flare triggering, such as plasma flows from low-altitude reconnection linked to emerging flux, and determine whether active region loops consist of a small number of strands or the hundreds predicted by magnetic reconnection-induced nanoflare heating models.

AI.tldr: The paper uses forward modeling to demonstrate that the upcoming SOLAR-C/EUVST mission will provide significantly better spatial, temporal, and temperature resolution than Hinode/EIS, enabling direct observation of fine-scale coronal structures, velocity gradients, and plasma flows relevant to flare triggering and coronal heating mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Solar flares and coronal heating involve processes across wide temperature ranges on small spatial scales and short timescales that are difficult to observe with current instrumentation like Hinode/EIS.
AI.method: Forward modeling of the SOLAR-C/EUVST-SW spectrograph (170-210 Å) applied to a 3D MHD simulation of a pre-flare active region, comparing its performance to Hinode/EIS.
AI.result: SOLAR-C/EUVST can resolve individual flux tubes and sharp velocity gradients in simulated active region loops that Hinode/EIS cannot; Doppler velocity accuracy will reach <1 km/s in active regions.
AI.conclusion: SOLAR-C/EUVST is expected to directly observe processes related to flare triggering (e.g., plasma flows from low-altitude reconnection linked to emerging flux) and distinguish between different models of coronal loop structure (few strands vs. hundreds predicted by nanoflare heating models).

title: Mapping Oscillatory Flows in a Giant Chromospheric Spiral
comment: Accepted for publication in The Astrophysical Journal (ApJ)
summary: The solar chromosphere is permeated by complex magnetic fields that guide plasma flows and energy into the corona. This work presents a detailed analysis of a unique, high-resolution observation of a giant chromospheric spiral structure that emerges due to a large magnetic pore, captured by the Swedish 1-m Solar Telescope (SST). A comprehensive data analysis pipeline is developed to automatically detect the edges of 2255 plasma flows (loops) that constitute the spiral, and these are used to extract the kinematics of flows propagating along the magnetic field. The analysis reveals three primary insights into the spiral's physics. First, magnetic curvature is correlated with oscillatory flow dynamics, i.e. regions of high loop curvature exhibit a statistically significant excess of higher-order oscillation modes compared to straighter loops; it is also correlated with higher intensity and longer periods. Second, spatial distribution of oscillation period shows an inverse trend, decreasing from $\sim$3.5 minutes in the pore to $\sim$3 minutes in the outer spiral arms. This is interpreted as a signature of the overlying trans-equatorial quadrupolar coronal loop system compressing the pore's field lines into a near-horizontal orientation, producing a period gradient that challenges the standard expanding canopy model. Finally, the emission signature confirms that oscillating threads represent localised channels of brightness that lie within cooler, absorbing loop material. This study provides the first statistical analysis of oscillatory flows in a large-scale spiral, probing energy flow through the chromosphere through curved magnetic structure.

AI.tldr: Analysis of a giant chromospheric spiral (2255 loops) from SST reveals that magnetic curvature correlates with higher oscillation modes and intensity, period decreases from ~3.5 min (pore) to ~3 min (outer arms), challenging the expanding canopy model, and oscillating threads are bright channels within cooler material.
AI.motivation: To understand how complex magnetic fields in the solar chromosphere guide plasma flows and energy transfer, specifically by analyzing a unique giant spiral structure emerging from a large magnetic pore.
AI.method: High-resolution SST observation; automated pipeline to detect edges of 2255 plasma loops; extraction of kinematics along magnetic field; statistical analysis of curvature, oscillation modes, periods, and intensity.
AI.result: (1) High loop curvature correlates with excess higher-order oscillation modes, higher intensity, and longer periods. (2) Oscillation period decreases from ~3.5 min (pore) to ~3 min (outer arms), interpreted as signature of overlying quadrupolar coronal loop system compressing field lines horizontally. (3) Oscillating threads are localized brightness channels within cooler absorbing material.
AI.conclusion: First statistical analysis of oscillatory flows in a large-scale spiral; reveals energy flow through curved chromospheric magnetic structure and challenges the standard expanding canopy model.

title: Unstable magnetospheric accretion on the T Tauri star TW Hya
comment: A&A in press (11 pages + 9p appendix, 14 figures, 4 tables)
summary: In this paper we present new spectropolarimetric and velocimetric observations of the prototypical classical T Tauri star TW Hya obtained with SPIRou at the Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope, expanding our previous monitoring over two new seasons (2024 and 2025). We confirm that the large-scale magnetic field of TW Hya varied with time, and find that it showed fluctuations on a timescale of about a year in addition to the longer term variations outlined in the previous study. Using Zeeman-Doppler imaging, we obtain that the large-scale field of TW Hya mostly consisted of a poloidal dipole of mean polar strength 0.83 kG, inclined at an average 17degr to the rotation axis. We also find that the radial velocities of TW Hya, once fully filtered from telluric contamination, were dominated by rotational modulation induced by activity, with residuals of 32 m/s rms. No signal from a putative close-in planet is found, with an upper limit on the planet mass ranging from 0.33 to 0.98 Mjup for distances of 0.053 to 0.41 au from the central star. Emission lines indicate that the mass accretion rate was equal to $10^{-8.33\pm0.20}$ Msun/yr on average, with peak-to-peak fluctuations by a factor of ~5 from season to season. This confirms that accretion onto TW Hya is unstable, with the magnetospheric gap carved by the large-scale field at the center of the disk extending on average no further than 33-40% of the corotation radius where the disk Keplerian angular velocity equals the rotation rate at the stellar surface.

AI.tldr: New SPIRou spectropolarimetric/velocimetric observations (2024-2025) of TW Hya confirm its large-scale magnetic field varies yearly (~0.83 kG poloidal dipole inclined ~17°), radial velocities are activity-dominated (32 m/s rms) with no close-in planet detected (upper limit 0.33-0.98 Mjup within 0.053-0.41 au), and accretion rate averages 10^-8.33 Msun/yr with ~5x seasonal fluctuations, indicating unstable accretion with the magnetospheric gap extending ≤33-40% of corotation radius.
AI.motivation: To extend monitoring of the prototypical classical T Tauri star TW Hya's magnetic field, accretion, and radial velocity variability over two new observing seasons, and to search for close-in planets.
AI.method: Spectropolarimetric and velocimetric observations with SPIRou at CFHT (2024-2025); Zeeman-Doppler imaging for magnetic field reconstruction; telluric filtering for radial velocity analysis.
AI.result: Large-scale field fluctuates on ~year timescale, dominated by a poloidal dipole (mean 0.83 kG, inclined ~17° to rotation axis); radial velocity residuals 32 m/s rms; no planet detected (mass upper limit 0.33-0.98 Mjup at 0.053-0.41 au); mean accretion rate 10^-8.33 Msun/yr with ~5x seasonal fluctuations.
AI.conclusion: TW Hya's accretion is unstable, with the magnetospheric gap extending no further than 33-40% of the corotation radius, and its large-scale magnetic field shows both yearly fluctuations and long-term variations.

title: STARSFLUX: an all-sky catalogue of absolute spectro-photometric calibrators from 0.3 to 30 $μ$m
comment: Submitted to Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
summary: Obtaining accurate fluxes for faint sources from the ground at near- and mid-infrared wavelengths is challenging because of rapidly varying atmospheric absorption, and is often limited by the lack of a nearby spectro-photometric calibrator. We present the STellar Absolute Reference Spectroscopic Flux Library (STARSFLUX), an all-sky catalogue of 64,484 calibrator spectra spanning 0.3--30 $μ$m, based on the Mid-infrared stellar Diameters and Fluxes compilation Catalogue (MDFC). STARSFLUX combines Gaia DR3 stellar parameters, multi-band photometry from space- and ground-based surveys, and synthetic NewEra PHOENIX atmosphere models. For each star, we fit the observed SED with an interpolated model spectrum, angular diameter, and extinction to produce an absolutely flux-calibrated spectrum. The derived angular diameters can also be used to calibrate interferometric observations. We validate the catalogue using three independent tests. First, STARSFLUX angular radii agree closely with Gaia DR3 radii, with a median $|ΔR|/R_{\rm Gaia,DR3} \simeq 4.8$ per cent. Second, integrated L-band (2.8--4.2 $μ$m) fluxes for 12 stars in common with the Cohen infrared standards agree with Cohen values with a mean absolute percentage difference of $4.7 \pm 2.6$ per cent. Third, comparison with Gaia DR3 BP/RP spectra shows that the near-UV/visible/near-IR absolute spectro-photometric fluxes agree to approximately 3 per cent. The FITS spectra are available at https://home.strw.leidenuniv.nl/~gamez/ and will be submitted to VizieR.

AI.tldr: STARSFLUX is an all-sky catalogue of 64,484 calibrator spectra from 0.3–30 μm, combining Gaia DR3 parameters, multi-band photometry, and synthetic PHOENIX models to produce absolutely flux-calibrated spectra validated to ~3–5% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Obtaining accurate fluxes for faint sources from the ground at near- and mid-infrared wavelengths is challenging due to rapidly varying atmospheric absorption and the lack of nearby spectro-photometric calibrators.
AI.method: STARSFLUX combines Gaia DR3 stellar parameters, multi-band photometry from space- and ground-based surveys, and synthetic NewEra PHOENIX atmosphere models. For each star, the observed SED is fitted with an interpolated model spectrum, angular diameter, and extinction to produce absolutely flux-calibrated spectra.
AI.result: Angular radii agree with Gaia DR3 with median |ΔR|/R_Gaia ≈ 4.8%; integrated L-band fluxes agree with Cohen standards with mean absolute percentage difference 4.7±2.6%; near-UV/visible/near-IR fluxes agree with Gaia BP/RP spectra to ~3%.
AI.conclusion: STARSFLUX provides a validated all-sky catalogue of 64,484 absolutely flux-calibrated stellar spectra from 0.3–30 μm, also providing angular diameters useful for calibrating interferometric observations.

title: Time-Resolved Connection between Starspots and Flares in Nearby Young Solar-type Stars Observed by TESS
comment: 10 pages, 11 figures, 2 tables. Accepted for publication in PASJ
summary: Superflares are energetic explosions on stellar surface with energies of 10^33-10^36 erg, significantly exceeding those of typical solar flares. While previous studies have suggested that these events are driven by magnetic energy stored in large starspots, the detailed time-resolved relationship between starspot area and flare activity on individual stars has remained unclear. In this paper, we investigate the time evolution of magnetic activity on three representative young solar-type stars (EK Draconis, DS Tucanae A, and V889 Herculis) using $\sim$7 years of photometric data from the Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS). We automatically detected stellar flares and derived the flare frequency, starspot area, and rotational period for each TESS sector covering ~27 days. As a result, we found that the flare frequency and starspot area vary significantly across sectors, although we could not identify any activity-cycle-like pattern. There is a positive correlation between the starspot area and flare occurrence frequency for all three targets and the power-law dependence is consistent among the stars. This result supports the physical picture that superflares on young solar-type stars are powered by magnetic energy stored in large starspots, analogous to solar flares, and that the energy release rate changes as the total stored magnetic energy varies. Furthermore, from the analysis of EK Draconis, we find a possible dependence of starspot area on rotation period, which may suggest that large starspots preferentially form at mid-latitudes. These findings demonstrate that the magnetic activity mechanisms established for the Sun extend to the extreme magnetic activity observed on young active stars.

AI.tldr: This study uses ~7 years of TESS photometric data on three young solar-type stars (EK Draconis, DS Tucanae A, V889 Herculis) to investigate the time-resolved relationship between starspot area and superflare activity. Results show a positive correlation between starspot area and flare frequency across sectors, consistent with the magnetic energy storage mechanism analogous to solar flares. Additionally, a possible dependence of starspot area on rotation period is found for EK Draconis.
AI.motivation: To clarify the detailed time-resolved relationship between starspot area and superflare activity on individual stars, which had remained unclear despite previous studies suggesting superflares are driven by magnetic energy stored in large starspots.
AI.method: Analysis of ~7 years of TESS photometric data on three young solar-type stars (EK Draconis, DS Tucanae A, V889 Herculis) with automated detection of stellar flares and derivation of flare frequency, starspot area, and rotational period for each ~27-day TESS sector.
AI.result: Flare frequency and starspot area vary significantly across sectors but without detectable activity-cycle-like patterns. A positive correlation exists between starspot area and flare occurrence frequency for all three targets with consistent power-law dependence. Possible dependence of starspot area on rotation period observed for EK Draconis, suggesting large starspots preferentially form at mid-latitudes.
