AI.conclusion: Superflares on young solar-type stars are powered by magnetic energy stored in large starspots, analogous to solar flares, with energy release rate changing as total stored magnetic energy varies. Magnetic activity mechanisms established for the Sun extend to the extreme magnetic activity observed on young active stars.

title: Direct Measurement of Diffusion Coefficients: Evidence for Diffusive Stochastic Heating in Collisionless Plasmas
comment: Accepted in PRL, 10 pages, 5 figures
summary: Open questions in collisionless plasma dissipation can be addressed using space-based observations in different astrophysical environments, with implications for both astrophysical and laboratory plasma systems. We study a low-$β$, highly imbalanced, sub-Alfvénic stream observed by Parker Solar Probe (PSP) to identify and distinguish between signatures of stochastic heating (SH) and resonant heating (RH) by parallel ion cyclotron waves (ICWs). Prior work studying this stream (Bowen et al., 2025) showed that the SH rate, accounting for intermittency, matched the amplitude of the local energy transfer (LET) rate while the RH rate did not. This comparison relied on a number of assumptions regarding the nature of the diffusive process, and the calculation of the LET rate. We introduce a novel technique of inverting the proton guiding center equation to empirically measure velocity-space diffusion coefficients using three-dimensional proton velocity distribution functions (VDFs), from the ion electrostatic analyzer (SPANi) on PSP. Measured diffusion coefficients are used to determine phase-space heating rates, leading to a calculation of a fully kinetic heating rate independent of assumptions made in prior work. We show that scale-dependent analytic expressions for SH via non-coherent fluctuations match the empirical measurements from PSP data, provided that we account for intermittency in the heating calculation. In contrast, the derived heating rates for SH that accounts for the effects of the helicity barrier, and heating rates for RH via $\parallel$-ICWs do not peak in the same region of velocity-space as the empirical measurements, nor reach the required magnitude. Our approach provides novel methodology to uniquely identify and constrain heating processes in collisionless plasmas, and shows evidence of a Fokker-Planck like diffusive process in the near-Sun solar wind.

AI.tldr: Using Parker Solar Probe data, this paper introduces a novel technique to invert the proton guiding center equation to empirically measure velocity-space diffusion coefficients from 3D proton VDFs. They show that stochastic heating (SH) via non-coherent fluctuations, when accounting for intermittency, matches empirical measurements, while resonant heating (RH) by parallel ion cyclotron waves does not, providing evidence for a Fokker-Planck-like diffusive process in the near-Sun solar wind.
AI.motivation: To address open questions in collisionless plasma dissipation by identifying and distinguishing between signatures of stochastic heating (SH) and resonant heating (RH) by parallel ion cyclotron waves (ICWs) in a low-β, highly imbalanced, sub-Alfvénic stream observed by PSP, and to overcome assumptions made in prior work (Bowen et al., 2025).
AI.method: Inverting the proton guiding center equation to empirically measure velocity-space diffusion coefficients from three-dimensional proton velocity distribution functions (VDFs) obtained by the SPANi instrument on PSP. These measured diffusion coefficients are then used to determine phase-space heating rates, yielding a fully kinetic heating rate independent of prior assumptions. Scale-dependent analytic expressions for SH via non-coherent fluctuations, SH accounting for the helicity barrier, and RH via parallel ICWs are compared against empirical measurements.
AI.result: 1) Scale-dependent analytic expressions for SH via non-coherent fluctuations match empirical measurements from PSP data when intermittency is accounted for. 2) Heating rates for SH that account for the helicity barrier and RH via parallel ICWs do not peak in the same velocity-space region as empirical measurements and do not reach the required magnitude. 3) Evidence of a Fokker-Planck-like diffusive process in the near-Sun solar wind.
AI.conclusion: The novel methodology provides a way to uniquely identify and constrain heating processes in collisionless plasmas. Stochastic heating via non-coherent fluctuations, rather than resonant heating by parallel ICWs or helicity barrier effects, is the dominant mechanism consistent with observations in this low-β, highly imbalanced stream.
